metadata.career_relevance: Zerodha runs on a 15-year-old ODIN trading platform — a Windows monolith that processes 10M+ trades daily. A big-bang rewrite (replace everything at once) is too risky: 6 months of downtime. Strangler Fig: run old and new systems in parallel. Route 1% of trades to new system, compare output, fix bugs, scale to 5%, 10%, 100%. After this topic, you can migrate any legacy system to microservices without downtime.

hook.real_world_problem:
Zerodha ODIN monolith migration:

```
BIG BANG REWRITE (what you DON'T do):
  Month 1-12: Build new system while old runs
  Month 13: Switch all users to new system
  Result: Bugs found in production with 10M users
           Old system gone, can't roll back
           3-day outage, ₹200 crore loss

STRANGLER FIG (the right way):
  Week 1: Deploy new Order Service alongside old ODIN
  Week 2: Route 1% of test orders to new service
  Week 3: Compare: new vs old output identical? → scale to 5%
  Month 2: 50% on new service (both running in parallel)
  Month 6: 100% on new service → retire ODIN component
  Result: Zero downtime, bugs found on 1% traffic, easy rollback
```
hook.what_you_will_build: A proxy router that splits traffic between legacy and new service based on feature flags.

concept_explanation.strangler_fig:
**Strangler Fig metaphor:**
```
The strangler fig vine grows around a host tree.
Gradually wraps and replaces the tree.
The tree eventually dies — the vine takes its shape.

Application:
  Legacy monolith = host tree
  New microservice = strangler fig vine
  Proxy layer = where old and new coexist
  Migration complete: legacy retired, new takes over
```

visual_aid.description: Migration timeline diagram. Left: Legacy ODIN monolith (large box). Right: New microservices (smaller boxes: OrderService, TradeService, PositionService). Center: Proxy/Router with feature flag showing traffic split percentages: Week 1: 0% new, Week 4: 10% new, Month 3: 50% new, Month 6: 100% new. Legacy box gradually shrinks as new boxes grow. Arrow from dark launch: 'shadow traffic' hits both systems. Comparison chart shows old vs new output equality verification.
visual_aid.alt_text: Strangler Fig Pattern: proxy routes traffic incrementally from legacy to new system

worked_example.scenario: Zerodha migrating order creation from ODIN to new Spring Boot microservice.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. StranglerFigRouter: feature flag decides old vs new
2. FeatureFlagService: percentage-based rollout
3. DarkLaunchService: run both, compare, return legacy result
4. @SpringBootTest: flag=false → legacy, flag=true → new service

step_by_step_solution.details: Phase 1 (Dark launch): route all traffic to old, also call new in background (shadow), compare outputs. Phase 2 (1% cutover): beta merchants use new service. Phase 3 (10-50%): gradual rollout with monitoring. Phase 4 (100%): all traffic on new service. Phase 5 (retire): delete legacy code. Never skip phases — each validates the new service before more traffic.

things_to_notice: Hash-based routing: Math.abs(merchantId.hashCode()) % 100 < rolloutPercent. This ensures the SAME merchant always goes to the same service (consistent experience — don't route same user to different systems on different requests).
Rollback: feature flag = 0% → all traffic instantly back to legacy. No deployment needed. This is the safety net for strangler fig migration.
Dark launch limitations: side effects in the new service (emails, payments) must be suppressed during dark launch. Route to new service in 'dry run' mode.
Monitoring during migration: track error rate, p99 latency, and business metrics (order fill rate) for both old and new. Alert if new service metrics diverge from legacy.

industry_examples.use_case: ODIN: 15-year-old Windows trading platform. Kite backend: new Spring Boot microservices. Migration: proxy at nginx level routes /api/orders to new service based on merchant whitelist. Dark launch: both process each order, compare fill prices (must match to 0.01 paise). 6-month migration with zero downtime and no user-visible issues.

applications.interview_relevance: 'How would you migrate a 10-year-old monolith to microservices without downtime?' → Strangler Fig pattern. (1) Wrap legacy in a proxy/router layer, (2) Build new microservice alongside, (3) Dark launch: call both, compare output, return legacy result, (4) Gradually route % of traffic to new service using feature flags, (5) Monitor: error rate, latency, business metrics, (6) Rollback: flip feature flag to 0% instantly. Never do a big-bang rewrite — too risky.
applications.real_world_workflow: Strangler Fig workflow: (1) identify candidate module to migrate, (2) add proxy layer (Spring filter or Nginx), (3) build new service with same API contract, (4) dark launch: shadow traffic, compare outputs, (5) feature flag: 1% → 5% → 10% → 50% → 100%, (6) monitor at each step, (7) retire legacy code after 100% stable.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Routing the same user to different services on consecutive requests (random routing). User sees order created in new service but then queries old service — order not found!
common_gaps.remediation: Hash-based routing: Math.abs(userId.hashCode()) % 100 < rolloutPercent. Deterministic — same user ALWAYS goes to same service during migration. Alternative: per-session sticky routing.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Random Math.random() < rolloutPercent routing — 'inconsistent user experience: order created in new service, queried from old service, 404 Not Found'

key_takeaways: Strangler Fig: incrementally replace legacy — never big-bang rewrite
Proxy layer: routes to old or new based on feature flag
Feature flag: percentage-based, deterministic (hash-based), instant rollback
Dark launch: shadow traffic to new system — compare outputs, user sees old result
Hash-based routing: same user → same service (consistency)
Rollback: set rollout=0% → instant back to legacy, no deploy needed
Monitor: error rate + latency + business metrics for both systems during migration

self_check_questions.question: Why is hash-based routing (merchantId.hashCode() % 100) better than random routing (Math.random()) for Strangler Fig migrations?
self_check_questions.answer: Random routing: each request independently decides which system to use. A merchant places an order → routed to new service → order created. Same merchant queries their orders → random routing sends them to old service → order not found (it's in the new service's DB). Result: data inconsistency, confused users, support tickets. Hash-based routing: Math.abs(merchantId.hashCode()) % 100 always produces the same number for the same merchantId. If rollout=10% and the hash is 7: ALL requests from that merchant go to new service. Consistent experience: data lives in one place (either old system DB or new service DB). Another benefit: easy rollback — lower the rolloutPercent to 0 → all merchants instantly back to legacy, no DB confusion.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Final Module 24 topic: Bulkhead & Sidecar Patterns — Resilience4j thread-pool isolation and Kubernetes sidecar containers for cross-cutting concerns.
